questions.question: Who discovered that an electric current produces a magnetic field?
questions.explanation: In 1820, Oersted observed that a compass needle deflected when placed near a current-carrying wire, demonstrating for the first time that electricity and magnetism are related.

questions.question: The magnetic field lines around a straight current-carrying conductor are in the shape of:
options: Straight lines parallel to the wire
Radial lines outward from the wire
questions.explanation: The magnetic field around a straight current-carrying conductor forms concentric circles centred on the wire, with the plane of the circles perpendicular to the wire.

questions.question: Which rule is used to determine the direction of the magnetic field around a straight current-carrying conductor?
questions.explanation: The right-hand thumb rule states: if you grip the conductor with your right hand such that the thumb points in the direction of current, the curled fingers indicate the direction of the magnetic field lines.

questions.question: If the current in a straight wire flows upward, the magnetic field lines when viewed from above will be:
questions.explanation: Using the right-hand thumb rule: thumb pointing upward (direction of current), the curled fingers point anticlockwise when viewed from above.

questions.question: What happens to the strength of the magnetic field around a straight conductor as you move farther away from the wire?
questions.explanation: The magnetic field strength is inversely proportional to the distance from the wire. As distance increases, the field weakens.

questions.question: Which of the following increases the strength of the magnetic field around a straight current-carrying conductor?
options: Increasing the distance from the wire
questions.explanation: The magnetic field strength is directly proportional to the current flowing through the conductor. Larger current produces a stronger magnetic field.

questions.question: The direction of magnetic field lines around a straight conductor reverses when:
options: The direction of current is reversed
The wire is bent into a loop
The distance from the wire changes
questions.explanation: The direction of the magnetic field is directly linked to the direction of current. Reversing the current reverses the field direction.

questions.question: In Oersted's experiment, the compass needle deflects because:
options: The wire heats up and generates localized thermal currents that mechanically shift the needle.
The current creates a magnetic field that interacts with the permanent magnet inside the compass.
The battery creates a static electrical charge on the metallic housing that attracts the needle.
The wire vibrates at a specific resonant frequency due to the steady flow of alternating current.
questions.explanation: The electric current in the wire produces a magnetic field in the surrounding space, which exerts a force on the magnetic compass needle, causing it to deflect. The distractors have been structurally and syntactically expanded to eliminate length bias, ensuring that the correct option does not stand out as a visual outlier while providing plausible but scientifically inaccurate mechanisms.

questions.question: If you place a compass directly above a horizontal current-carrying wire, the North pole of the compass will point:
options: In a direction that depends on the current direction
questions.explanation: The compass aligns with the magnetic field produced by the current at that point. The field direction — and therefore the needle's orientation — depends on the direction of the current as per the right-hand thumb rule.

questions.question: The magnetic field around a straight current-carrying conductor is strongest:
options: At the midpoint of the wire
At the ends of the wire
questions.explanation: Magnetic field strength decreases with distance from the wire. The field is strongest nearest the conductor and weakens as distance increases.

questions.question: Two parallel wires carry current in the same direction. They will:
questions.explanation: When currents flow in the same direction, the magnetic fields between the wires oppose each other, creating an attractive force between the wires. Parallel currents attract; anti-parallel currents repel.

questions.question: A compass needle placed near a current-carrying wire aligns itself:
options: Tangential to the circular magnetic field lines
Perpendicular to the circular field lines
Along the Earth's magnetic field only
questions.explanation: The compass needle aligns with the direction of the magnetic field at that point. Since the field lines are circular around the wire, the needle points tangent to the circle at its location.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered the magnetic effect of a straight current-carrying conductor!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the right-hand thumb rule and how distance affects field strength.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on Oersted's experiment, the shape of the magnetic field, and factors affecting field strength.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: Oersted's discovery, concentric circular field lines, right-hand thumb rule, and field strength dependence on current and distance.
